{"path": "workspace/security_comparison.txt", "content": "================================================================================
          Zero Trust Security vs. Defense in Depth: A Comparative Analysis
================================================================================

1. OVERVIEW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST SECURITY
    Zero Trust is a security model built on the principle of "never trust, always
    verify." It assumes that threats can exist both outside and inside the
    network perimeter, and therefore no user, device, or application should be
    trusted by default — even if they are already inside the network.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH
    Defense in Depth is a layered security strategy that deploys multiple,
    overlapping defensive controls across an organization's infrastructure.
    The idea is that if one layer fails, the next layer continues to provide
    protection. It originates from military strategy and has been adapted to
    information security.


2. CORE PRINCIPLES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST PRINCIPLES:
    a) Verify Explicitly — Authenticate and authorize every access request based
       on all available data points (user identity, device health, location,
       data classification, anomalies, etc.).
    b) Least Privilege Access — Grant only the minimum level of access necessary
       for a user or system to perform its function. Use Just-In-Time (JIT) and
       Just-Enough-Access (JEA) models.
    c) Assume Breach — Operate as if the network is already compromised.
       Segment networks, encrypt everywhere, use continuous monitoring, and
       minimize blast radius.
    d) Micro-Segmentation — Break the network into small, isolated zones. Each
       zone requires separate authentication and authorization.
    e) Continuous Verification — Trust is never permanent. Sessions are
       continuously re-evaluated based on behavior, device posture, and context.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH PRINCIPLES:
    a) Layered Controls — Deploy security at multiple levels: physical,
       network, host, application, and data.
    b) Redundancy — No single control is relied upon. Overlapping controls
       compensate for the failure of any one layer.
    c) Diversity of Controls — Use different types of controls (preventive,
       detective, corrective, deterrent, compensating) from different vendors
       or technologies to avoid single points of failure.
    d) Perimeter-First Mindset (Traditional) — Historically emphasizes a strong
       outer perimeter (firewalls, IDS/IPS) with progressively weaker internal
       controls, though modern implementations have evolved.


3. KEY DIFFERENCES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| ASPECT                      | ZERO TRUST                      | DEFENSE IN DEPTH                |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Philosophy                  | Trust nothing, verify           | Layer everything, hope one       |
|                             | everything                      | layer stops the threat           |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Network Assumption          | Network is always hostile;      | Internal network is safer than   |
|                             | no implicit trust based on      | external; perimeter matters      |
|                             | network location                |                                  |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Primary Focus               | Identity, data, and access      | Infrastructure and network       |
|                             | governance                      | topology                         |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Access Control              | Dynamic, context-aware,         | Often static, role-based,        |
|                             | continuous                      | session-based                    |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Perimeter Concept           | Perimeter is irrelevant;        | Perimeter is the first and most  |
|                             | micro-perimeters around each    | critical line of defense         |
|                             | resource                        |                                  |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Trust Model                 | Trust is a vulnerability;       | Trust is granted once inside     |
|                             | no default trust                | the perimeter (in traditional    |
|                             |                                 | implementations)                 |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Lateral Movement            | Designed to prevent lateral     | May allow lateral movement if    |
|                             | movement via micro-segmentation | perimeter is breached            |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Implementation Complexity   | High — requires re-architecting | Moderate — can be built          |
|                             | identity, network, and apps     | incrementally on existing infra  |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Technology Emphasis         | IAM, MFA, ZTNA, CASB, D

{"content": "================================================================================
          Zero Trust Security vs. Defense in Depth: A Comparative Analysis
================================================================================

1. OVERVIEW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST SECURITY
    Zero Trust is a security model built on the principle of "never trust, always
    verify." It assumes that threats can exist both outside and inside the
    network perimeter, and therefore no user, device, or application should be
    trusted by default — even if they are already inside the network.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH
    Defense in Depth is a layered security strategy that deploys multiple,
    overlapping defensive controls across an organization's infrastructure.
    The idea is that if one layer fails, the next layer continues to provide
    protection. It originates from military strategy and has been adapted to
    information security.


2. CORE PRINCIPLES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST PRINCIPLES:
    a) Verify Explicitly — Authenticate and authorize every access request based
       on all available data points (user identity, device health, location,
       data classification, anomalies, etc.).
    b) Least Privilege Access — Grant only the minimum level of access necessary
       for a user or system to perform its function. Use Just-In-Time (JIT) and
       Just-Enough-Access (JEA) models.
    c) Assume Breach — Operate as if the network is already compromised.
       Segment networks, encrypt everywhere, use continuous monitoring, and
       minimize blast radius.
    d) Micro-Segmentation — Break the network into small, isolated zones. Each
       zone requires separate authentication and authorization.
    e) Continuous Verification — Trust is never permanent. Sessions are
       continuously re-evaluated based on behavior, device posture, and context.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH PRINCIPLES:
    a) Layered Controls — Deploy security at multiple levels: physical,
       network, host, application, and data.
    b) Redundancy — No single control is relied upon. Overlapping controls
       compensate for the failure of any one layer.
    c) Diversity of Controls — Use different types of controls (preventive,
       detective, corrective, deterrent, compensating) from different vendors
       or technologies to avoid single points of failure.
    d) Perimeter-First Mindset (Traditional) — Historically emphasizes a strong
       outer perimeter (firewalls, IDS/IPS) with progressively weaker internal
       controls, though modern implementations have evolved.


3. KEY DIFFERENCES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| ASPECT                      | ZERO TRUST                      | DEFENSE IN DEPTH                |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Philosophy                  | Trust nothing, verify           | Layer everything, hope one       |
|                             | everything                      | layer stops the threat           |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Network Assumption          | Network is always hostile;      | Internal network is safer than   |
|                             | no implicit trust based on      | external; perimeter matters      |
|                             | network location                |                                  |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Primary Focus               | Identity, data, and access      | Infrastructure and network       |
|                             | governance                      | topology                         |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Access Control              | Dynamic, context-aware,         | Often static, role-based,        |
|                             | continuous                      | session-based                    |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Perimeter Concept           | Perimeter is irrelevant;        | Perimeter is the first and most  |
|                             | micro-perimeters around each    | critical line of defense         |
|                             | resource                        |                                  |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Trust Model                 | Trust is a vulnerability;       | Trust is granted once inside     |
|                             | no default trust                | the perimeter (in traditional    |
|                             |                                 | implementations)                 |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Lateral Movement            | Designed to prevent lateral     | May allow lateral movement if    |
|                             | movement via micro-segmentation | perimeter is breached            |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Implementation Complexity   | High — requires re-architecting | Moderate — can be built          |
|                             | identity, network, and apps     | incrementally on existing infra  |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Technology Emphasis         | IAM, MFA, ZTNA, CASB, DLP,      | Firewalls, IDS/IPS, antivirus,   |
|                             | micro-segmentation, SASE        | VPNs, NAC, SIEM, WAF             |
+-----------------------------+--------------------------------
